coredata.dc:title: Solid-phase synthesis of red dual-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots for the detection of Cu2+ and glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Fangming
Zhou, Peng
Duan, Chunying
coredata.dc:description: Red dual-emissive carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by a novel and facile solvent-free carbonization method using O-phenylenediamine (OPD) as precursor and Al(NO3)3·9H2O as assistant. The prepared CDs manifesting intriguing dual-emission behavior at 600 nm and 650 nm demonstrate good fluorescence quenching rate (99%) on Cu2+, in which the linear range was 6–33 μM and detection limit was 0.145 μM. In addition, the CDs-Cu2+ system shows excellent glutathione (GSH) turn-on detection in the range of 19–52 μM, where a red-shift to 650 nm and 700 nm was observed and the maximum value of F/F0 is 5.8. Reasonable quenching mechanism was speculated to be the combination of static and dynamic quenching. Cytotoxicity measurements displayed good biocompatibility of CDs and cell viability was higher than 83% even under the highest concentration of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Solid-phase synthesis
Copper ion detection
Glutathione detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence turn-off determination of Cu2+ and mechanism explanation
Determination of Cu2+ and GSH in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was financially supported by the National Major Scientific Instruments and Equipment Develop Project of China ( 2012YQ090197 ) and the National Science Foundation of China ( 21777017 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs on an ultra-thin film. Inset(right): HR-TEM. (B) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (C) Raman spectrum of CDs under 633 nm laser excitation. (D) XRD patterns of CDs.
(A) XPS full survey scan of CDs. (B) High resolution of C1s peaks of CDs. (C) High resolution of N1s peaks of CDs. (D) High resolution of O1s peaks of CDs.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under excitation wavelength ranging from 400 nm to 540 nm. (B) UV-Vis and FL spectra of CDs.

(A) Fluorescence response of CDs in the presence of different concentration of Cu2+. (B) Plot of fluorescence quenching value F0/F with the Cu2+ concentration from 0 μM to 77 μM, inset: linear fitting curve of (F0-F)/F0 versus the concentration of Cu2+ range from 6 to 33 μM. (C) Fluorescence responses of CDs over various ions. (D) Fluorescence stability studies of CDs in different pH solutions.
(A) Fluorescence lifetime decay tests and fit results of CDs and CDs-Cu2+. (B) Sensing mechanism of fluorescence quenching detection towards Cu2+.

(A) Fluorescence responses of CDs-Cu2+ solution in the presence of various contents of GSH. (B) The concentration dependent coefficient and its linear relation for the detection of GSH, the inset offers the corresponding line relationship from 19 to 52 μM. (C) Selectivity tests of different amino acid (D) Zeta potential value of CDs, CDs + Cu2+, CDs + Cu2++GSH.

(A) Cellular viability of A549 cells incubated with CDs in the range of 10–50 μg/ml. Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of H2030 cells incubated with CDs for 12 h under brightfield (B), under darkfield (C), incubated with CDs-Cu2+ for 12 h (D).
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of CDs (A) and its application for Cu2+ and GSH detection (B).
Determination of Cu2+ in water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Solid-phase synthesis of red dual-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots for the detection of Cu2+ and glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red dual-emissive carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by a novel and facile solvent-free carbonization method using O-phenylenediamine (OPD) as precursor and Al(NO3)3·9H2O as assistant. The prepared CDs manifesting intriguing dual-emission behavior at 600 nm and 650 nm demonstrate good fluorescence quenching rate (99%) on Cu2+, in which the linear range was 6–33 μM and detection limit was 0.145 μM. In addition, the CDs-Cu2+ system shows excellent glutathione (GSH) turn-on detection in the range of 19–52 μM, where a red-shift to 650 nm and 700 nm was observed and the maximum value of F/F0 is 5.8. Reasonable quenching mechanism was speculated to be the combination of static and dynamic quenching. Cytotoxicity measurements displayed good biocompatibility of CDs and cell viability was higher than 83% even under the highest concentration of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are relatively new carbon-based nanomaterials, usually smaller than 20 nm in size, and contain some surface passivation layers [1]. CDs have currently been treated as a promising fluorescent material due to their alluring fluorescent properties, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, easy functionalization, etc [2–6]. Recently, the heavy-metal-free fluorescent material has been widely applied in drug delivery [7,8], cell imaging [9,10], photodynamic therapy [7], light-emitting diodes [11,12], and fluorescent detection [13,14]. Heteroatom doping will influence the structure, optical and physicochemical properties of carbon nanomaterials. CDs containing heteroatoms such as boron [15,16], nitrogen [17–19], phosphorus [17] and fluorine [20,21] have been developed as fluorescent sensors with good performance. Various synthesis methods of CDs have been developed for the preparation of CDs, such as electrochemical oxidation, laser/chemical ablation, thermal decomposition, arc discharge, ultrasonication, microwave pyrolysis, hydrothermal and solvothermal and so on. These methods can be summarized into two categories: “bottom-up” from small molecular and “top-down” breaking from complex particles [22]. The former has good controllability, but the purification process is cumbersome, while the latter usually involves stringent reaction conditions, such as concentrated acids/alkali or toxic organic solvents. Solid-phase synthesis of CDs has drew much attention in recent years due to its solvent-free, high product yield and environmentally friendly advantages. Chen et al. proposed a solid-phase synthesis of blue-emitting fluorescent nitrogen-doped CDs for sensitive ferric ions sensing using PEG-diamine and citric acid [23]. Lu et al. obtained a blue-emitting CDs for biosensing and imaging of Cu2+ in liver cells using glucose and octadecylamine as carbon sources [24]. Zhao et al. synthesized yellow-emitting hydrophobic CDs by mixing citric acid and L-cysteine and dicyclohexyl-carbodiimide as dehydrating agent [25]. Song et al. prepared a red luminescent CD for pH sensing by directly calcining 1,2,4-triaminobenzene [26] However, there are few reports on fluorescent probes with red-emitting carbon dots by a facile method.

Copper ion is a trace element in human body and plays an important role in a number of biological process, including iron absorption, haemopoiesis, various enzyme-catalyzed and redox reactions [27]. Abnormal copper ion content can cause vomiting, drowsiness, increased blood pressure and respiratory rate, liver damage, neurotoxicity, neurodegenerative diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), Huntington's disease (HD), Wilson disease, etc. In addition, copper ions are also heavy metal pollutants in the environment [28]. Trace amounts of copper ions will pollute drinking water and bare soil, thereby endangering human health for a long time. Glutathione (GSH, γ-glutamyl-cysteyl-glycine) is the most abundant biothiol in cells, and it plays a pivotal role in biological process from anti-oxidation defense to immunity maintenance. Unusual GSH levels are associated with many diseases, such as immune dysfunctions, liver disease and many types of cancer [29]. Owing to these importance, GSH and copper ions have become one of the targets of numerous studies aimed at developing fluorescent probes and chemical sensors [29,30]. Hu reported a method for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of Cu2+ and GSH by CDs emission at 446 nm, and it was fabricated through one-pot facile hydrothermal treatment using o-phenylenediamine and citric acid as precursors [28]. Shuang developed a dual emission fluorescent CDs through a one-step hydrothermal procedure using alizarin carmine. According to the ratio fluorescence changes at 430 and 642 nm, the obtained CD can determine GSH selectively and sensitively [31]. Dong synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots by a one-step pyrolysis method using D-glucose and L-asparagine as precursors. The blue probe showed high sensitivity toward copper ionsand GSH with a low detection limit of 3.62 × 10−4 μM and 6.32 × 10−4 μM, respectively [32]. However, the main limitation of most existing CDs is that their fluorescence emission is concentrated in the shorter wavelength region. Therefore, it is a huge challenge to obtain long-wavelength emission CD, which has broad application prospects in the field of sensing and biological imaging.

Here, we prepared red dual-emissive CDs by a facile non-solvent solid-carbonization method from OPD and Al(NO3)3·9H2O, as illustrated in Scheme 1 A. Then an on–off-on switch fluorescent assay was developed for copper ion and GSH detection (Scheme 1B). The fluorescence of the CDs was quenched by 99% mainly due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) to Cu2+. The linear range of Cu2+ sensing was 6 μM-33 μM and the detection limit was 0.145 μM. Moreover, CD-Cu2+ system displayed excellent fluorescence enhancement along with a red-shift phenomenon when adding GSH. Furthermore, the dual-emission CDs-based sensing strategy has been used to determine Cu2+ and GSH levels in real samples.

The regents used in this work are O-phenylenediamine (OPD), Aluminum nitrate nonahydrate, dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), absolute ethyl alcohol, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, ZnCl2, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Cd (NO3)2·4H2O, BaCl2, CaCl2·2H2O, NaCl, AgNO3, MgCl2·6H2O, KCl, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O and CuSO4·5H2O, reduced GSH, alanine (Ala), lysine (Lys), arginine (Arg), valine (Val), aspartic acid (AA), tryptophan (Trp), glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys), glucose (Glu), human serum and CCK-8 kit which were purchased from several chemical works (Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. in China, Tianjin Guangfu technology development Co. Ltd., Sino pharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., Sigma Aldrich and so on), and all these were used as received without any further purification.

The morphology of CDs was analyzed with a Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, TECNAI G2 20). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using Thermo ESCALAB XI + X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The radicals of the CDs were characterized by a Bruker VERTEX-70 Fourier transformation infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained with a Rigaku D/Max 2400 automatic powder X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The Raman spectrum was measured using Multi-wavelength Laser Confocal In-Situ High-resolution Raman Imaging System (LabRAM HR Evolution). Steady state and time-resolved PL spectra were collected respectively on Edinburgh instrument FLS1000 and FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer under excitation wavelength of 530 nm. UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy was performed on TU-1900 spectrophotometer. The element analysis experiment was conducted by using a Vario EL III elemental analyzer. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed in a typical three-electrode cell system using an Ag/Ag+ as reference electrode, platinum plate as the counter electrode, and the sample modified glassy carbon as the working electrode. The electrochemical workstation was a CHI 660E potentionstat. For the cell culture experiments, A549 cells and H2030 cells were obtained from the Dalian Institute of Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Dalian, People’s Republic of China). They were incubated in Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RMPI) 1640 medium containing 10% fatal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotics (penicillin–streptomycin, 10,000 U·mL−1), respectively. Then the cells were cultured in 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. The absorbance in the cytotoxicity measurements was measured using a microplate reader (Spectramax I3X). The cellular imaging pictures were taken at a Leica DMi8 fluorescence inverted microscope.

CDs were prepared by a one-step pyrolysis method, which was inspired by the reported work [33] and make a slight improvement. First, 540.7 mg(5 mM) OPDA and 123.8 mg (0.33 mM) Al(NO3)3·9H2O were blended together and then added to the mortar, grinded to powder as hard as possible for 10 min. Then the mixture of powder was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 ml), heated at 200 °C for 12 h, and then cooled to room temperature, to yield a black mixture. Subsequently, 8 ml deionized water was added to the mixture, then centrifuged under 10000 rpm for 5 min. Repeated twice to remove water-soluble aluminum ion. After centrifugation, the remnants were dried in an oven with 60 °C. In the end, the dried powders thus obtained were dispersed into mixed solutions of ethanol with water in different proportions to achieve red dual-emissive CDs for further use and characterizations.

For the titration of Cu2+, gradually added equal volume of freshly prepared solution of Cu(NO3)2 into 0.1 mg/ml the obtained CDs dispersed in ethanol/water. For the detection of GSH, different volumes of freshly prepared 20 mg/ml GSH were added into mixed solutions of CD–Cu2+ system and then incubated for 1 h at 37 °C. After that, these obtained solutions were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for three minutes before the spectral measurements in order to discard insoluble matter. Then we use 10 times diluted human serum to determine the potential practical applications of CDs in detecting GSH.

The cytotoxicity was measured via CCK-8 assay. A549 cells were cultured on 96-well plates. After 24 h, CDs with different concentrations (10–50 µg/mL) dispersed in DMSO (concentration below 1‰) were added into each well. After further incubation for 24 h, 10 µL of the CCK-8 solution was added to each well of the plate. The absorbance was measured using a microplate reader after 2 h. The cell viabilities (%) were calculated as follows:
(1)
Cell
V i a b i l i t y
%
= O
D
treated
/ O
D
control
where OD Control was tested in the absence of CDs, and OD treated was measured in the presence of CDs, respectively.

H2030 cells were cultured in a glass–bottom culture dish in RPMI supplemented with 10% FBS at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator. The suspension of CDs (50 µg·mL−1) in cell culture medium was sonicated for 10 min to promote uniform distribution. Then 1 ml suspension was added to the dish and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. Before observing cells with a fluorescence microscope, the excess CDs were removed by washing with warm isosmotic saline solution (140 mM NaCl, 3.5 mM KCl, and 10 mM glucose).

The morphology of CDs was characterized by TEM. As demonstrated in Fig. 1 A, the as-prepared CDs were spherical and well monodispersed. The corresponding particle size is at around 10 nm. HRTEM of CDs shows a lattice fringe distance of 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (002) diffraction facets of graphite-like multilayer structure. The surface composition and element analysis of CDs were carried out by the FTIR, XRD, Raman and XPS. Fig. 1B demonstrates the FTIR spectrum of CDs, from which bending vibrations of C–H (615 cm−1 and 745 cm−1), stretching vibrations of C–O (1077 cm−1 and 1127 cm−1), aromatic C–N (1308 cm−1), CC (1479 cm−1), CO/CN (1585 and 1619 cm−1), H–CO (2820 cm−1) and O–H/N–H (3071 cm−1 and 3800 cm−1) can be observed. Aromatic C–N stretching vibration at 1308 cm−1 indicates the form of N-containing pyridinic heterocyclic rings inside the CDs. Raman spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1C) shows the characteristic D band at 1363 cm−1 and G band at 1557 cm−1. The D band is attributed to the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in terminal planes of disordered graphite, and the G band is related to the E2g mode of graphite due to the vibration of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms [34]. Notably, the intensity ratio ID/IG is about 9, which confirms the high degree of graphitization and high level of amorphous state on the surface of CDs caused by plentiful disorderly functionalization such as amino and carboxyl. And two additional defect modes discovered in this study, namely, D2 at 1447 cm−1 and D3 at 1476 cm−1, which originate from carbon bond vibrational coupling deviations in the vicinity of the sp3 defect site, such as NH2, OC–OH, etc [35]. Fig. 1D shows the XRD pattern of CDs, which has a relatively sharp peak of (002) at about 26.2°, corresponding to a layer spacing of 3.4 Å in the strongly crystalline graphite-like multilayer structure, which suggests CDs possess high extent of graphitization and large effective conjugation system.

XPS measurements were employed to further uncover the chemical environment and composition, as depicted in Fig. 2 . The predominant C1s peak at ~ 284 eV, N1s peak at ~ 399 eV, and O1s peak at ~ 531 eV are observed in the full survey XPS spectra of CDs (Fig. 2A). The high-resolution XPS spectra are shown in Fig. 2B, 2C, 2D. The C1s envelop can be split into four Lorentzian–Gaussian peaks. Generally, the peak centered at 284.4 eV is attributed to graphitic sp2 C (C–C/CC), the peak located in 285.6 eV is assigned to C–N/C-O, the peak at 287.2 eV corresponds to CO, the peak at 288.9 eV results from O–CO. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectra can be fitted into four peaks at 397.68 eV, 398.90 eV, 400.28 eV, and 401.40 eV, which correspond to the pyridinic N, amino N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectra can be decomposed into three peaks at 530.7 eV, 531.5 eV and 532.0 eV, which are the results of the existing CO, Ph–CO and C–O, respectively. The content ratio of C/N is 4.35, then the atom number of C:N:O is approximately calculated to be 12:2:1, which can be used to speculate the simplest molecular formula and chemical structure.

In order to find out more detailed optical and electrical properties of CDs, we then conducted fluorescence and UV–Vis spectrum tests. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the excitation-independent emission phenomenon could be observed when excited from 400 nm to 550 nm at the interval of 10 nm. As the excitation wavelength increases from 400 nm to 530 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity increases gradually, then appears the opposite trend as λex grows from 540 nm to longer wavelength. The maximum emission of CDs is located in 600 nm under 530 nm and 540 nm excitation, and the secondary emission peak at about 650 nm shows consistency with the peak at 600 nm. The excitation-independent characteristic probably originates from the π* to n-state energy levels transition of electrons, where n-state energy is the non-bonding state energy level caused by O/N functionalities on the surface of CDs [36]. We also conducted stability tests, as shown in Fig. S1, when exposed to visible light at room temperature for four months, the fluorescence intensity kept almost the same as before. The UV–Vis spectrum, as shown in Fig. 3B, displays three major absorption peaks; the strongly intense absorption peak at 300 nm and the second peak at 360 nm were assigned to the π–π* transitions of the CC in the core of the CDs, while the weakly absorption scope from 500 nm to 570 nm was attributed to the n–π* transitions of the surface states containing CN/CO and C–O structures. As we all know, the absorption peak due to π–π* transitions of the CC has a relation to the conjugation system. As the conjugation system grows larger, the transition energy becomes smaller, and the absorption moves toward the long wavelength. Therefore, the UV–Vis spectrum shows the successful formation of large conjugation system inside CDs.

Based on the above results, we believe that during the grinding process using OPD and Al(NO3)3·9H2O, aluminum ions perform as electron–deficient centers, and benzene ring act as electron–rich centers. Meanwhile, –NH2 on OPD and NO3 – have critical role in the N/O functionalization of CDs, which is closely related to the fluorescence change caused by the oxidation of the CDs surface. Then CDs experienced a growth through a facial in-situ heating carbonization method without any organic solvents adding, which saves the synthesis time of CDs meanwhile without any organic solvents waste. All these results above show that well-functionalized red dual-emissive CDs were prepared successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence turn-off determination of Cu2+ and mechanism explanation
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Cu2+ was conducted in a solution consisting of 0.1 mg/ml CDs. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, with the addition of Cu2+ ranging from 0 to 77 μM, the fluorescence of CDs was gradually quenched. The relationship between the quenching rate (F0-F)/F0 at 600 nm and Cu2+ concentration was displayed in Fig. 4B, in which the linear relationship in the region of 6 μM-33 μM was fitted to be y = 0.03713 + 0.01944*x, R2 = 0.99. Besides, CDs also showed good linear relationship on Cu2+ at a higher concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.145 μM according to the formula LOD = 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation for the fluorescence intensity of the blank solution, and k is the slope of the fitted curve [37]. Our detection method is more effective with wider linear range when compared with others (Table S2). Then the selectivity tests were conducted, as shown in Fig. 4C. CDs sensor was quenched by Cu2+, and the max quenching rate was calculated to be 99.2%. Meanwhile, the FL intensity of CDs was influenced by Al3+, Fe3+ in some degree, while the quenched fluorescence by them recovered almost to the original level after the addition of EDTA. However, the fluorescence of CDs-Cu2+ showed no recovery in addition of EDTA. Therefore, with the assistance of EDTA, CDs performed well as an excellent sensor towards Cu2+ with good selectivity and high sensitivity. Then we obtained the fluorescence emission intensity under different pH environment ranging from 1 to 14, as shown in Fig. 4D, which shows consistent fluorescent emission intensity of CDs within the range of pH 5–9. Therefore, under this circumstance, we could say any obvious decline in emission intensity was caused by quencher rather than pH change.

To find out the quenching mechanism, we also conducted test by changing the solvent to 0.2 M sulfuric acid. As depicted in Fig. S2, when adding the same dose of Cu2+ as before to the solution, there was no reduction in the fluorescence intensity of CDs at all, which indicated the protonation of functional group such as –NH2 can greatly inhibit the quenching behavior. In detail, the lone pairs of electrons on the amino on the surface of CDs form a coordination bond with the empty orbital of the H+, thus CDs cannot provide electrons to the copper ion. Fluorescence decay test and CV tests also verified our suspect. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs without/with Cu2+ was calculated to be 4.14 ns/3.66 ns (τ0/τ) respectively, according to the double-exponential fitting results [38].

)

As we all know, the typical quenching mechanisms were a result of static quenching or dynamic quenching in nature. We see that the lifetime of CDs has a 12% reduction. Therefore, these results indicate a dynamic quenching process. PET occurs between the CDs and Cu2+ and there should be both decrease in lifetime and the energy gap between the CDs and the quencher [39], which was confirmed by the following CV measurements, as illustrated in Fig. S3. The shape of the CV pattern remains unchanged after three cycles, indicating that CDs were electrochemically stable. The redox potential of CDs is −0.867 eV, then energy level of lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (ELUMO ) is calculated to be −3.53 eV based on formula (3). What’s more, the energy band is estimated to be 2.34 eV according to the UV–Vis absorption edge and empirical formula (4). Then energy level of the highest occupied molecular orbital (E HOMO) was reckoned to be −5.87 eV on the basis of formula (5) [40].
(3)
E
LUMO
= -
(
E
red
+ 4.4 )
(4)
E g
= 1240 /
λ
max
(5)
E g
=
E
LUMO
-
E
HOMO

As illustrated in Fig. 5B, electrons in the excited state of CDs tend to be transferred to the d orbitals of Cu2+, then the radiative transition of electrons is suppressed, leading to fluorescence quenching of CDs. Based on the above discussion, we speculated the quenching mechanism to be a combination of static and dynamic quenching.

To estimate the feasibility of CDs-Cu2+ system towards GSH detection, we conducted analysis of GSH with diverse concentrations ranging from 0 μM to 52 μM. As illustrated in Fig. 6 A, with the gradual increase amount of GSH, the quenched CDs–Cu2+ system got a significant gradual fluorescence enhancement and the maximum fluorescence enhancement rate (F/F0) is 580%. The illustration in Fig. 6B shows the linear plot for the quantitative analysis of GSH between F/F0 and 1/[GSH], which can be fitted to y = −137.5x + 8.76, R2 = 0.98. We could see another interesting phenomenon that there is not only fluorescence enhancement but also accompanied by a red shift in dual–emissive–peak. Fig. 6C displayed the good selectivity of GSH, that is to say, CD–Cu2+ system showed no fluorescence signal change towards other amino acid except GSH.

Moreover, zeta-potential is a measure of degree of electrostatic repulsion between adjacent similar charged particle in dispersed system. The zeta potential of 0.02 mg/ml CDs was −5.74 mV and CDs–Cu2+ was 12.5 mV, as showed in Fig. 6D, the zeta potential value of CD–Cu2+–GSH was 3.79 mV. Cu2+ has a high affinity with CDs, when chelated with multiple functional groups (–COOH, –NH2), the zeta potential changes from positive to negative. While adding GSH to the CD–Cu2+ system, the potential value was turned down owing to the interaction between Cu2+ and GSH. As we know, Cu2+ can act as a catalyst during the oxidation of GSH, while in the absence of Cu2+, GSH cannot be auto-oxidized by oxygen. Based on the analysis above, we conjecture that the oxidation of states increases the number of electrons relaxing to emit at 650 nm and 700 nm, then facilitating the rise in fluorescence intensity with increasing GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ and GSH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore potential applications of the established methods towards Cu2+ and GSH detection, we then implemented determination of Cu2+ in water samples and GSH in human serum. The Cu2+ recovery experiments were carried out by adding different amounts of Cu2+-containing tap water and sea water into 0.1 mg/ml CDs solution, which were shown in Table1 . The recovery of Cu2+ was between 99.85% and 107.7% for water samples with RSD around 0.35% to 2.93%. The GSH recovery tests were conducted by spiking different amounts of GSH into 10-fold diluted human serum and the results were listed in Table S1. The recoveries of all human serum samples were in the range of 96.28% to 105.6%. The above results strongly confirm the application of the proposed methods in real sample detection.

The toxicity of CDs was a general concern in the light of their potential applications in cellular imaging and bioassay. Herein, cytotoxicity studies were conducted, as depicted in Fig. 7 A, to our delight, though the cellular viability gradually decreased with the increase in the concentration of CDs, fortunately, the survival rate was higher than 83% even under the highest concentration (50 μg/ml) of CDs. This result shows that it is suitable for CDs to be applied in bioimaging of live cells or other biomedical fields.

In addition, cell imaging tests were then carried out to demonstrate the good optical properties in complex biosystem. Human H2030 cells were used to incubated with 50 μg/ml CDs for 12 h and then observed by Leica DMi8 fluorescence inverted microscope. As shown in Fig. 7B, 7C, H2030 cells almost maintained good morphologies under brightfield (Fig. 7B) and darkfield (Fig. 7C). In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched, and no visible fluorescence was observed in the dark field, as shown in Fig. 7D, which indicates that CDs can potentially be used to detect abnormal amounts of copper ion in living cells.

In summary, CDs were synthesized through a facile solid-phase solvent-free method from OPD. The resultant CDs showed excitation-independent dual-emission fluorescence property and had the advantages of low toxicity and biocompatibility. The CDs with dual emission peaks(600 nm and 650 nm)under a single excitation were employed for “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and GSH with high sensitivity and selectivity. The CDs showed excellent detection capability towards Cu2+ over a wide concentration range of 6–33 μM with LOD of 0.145 μM. Additionally, the CDs-Cu2+ system was utilized as GSH sensor within the range of 19–52 μM accompanied with a red-shift to 650 nm and 700 nm, in which the maximum value of F/F0 is 5.8. The above sensors had proved practical application in real water sample for Cu2+ and in human serum for GSH with good recovery. The mechanism of quenching in the existence of Cu2+ was speculated to be the coexistence of dynamic and static quenching and the GSH turn-on detection was attributed to be the oxidation of GSH with the catalysis of Cu2+. In the future, with our joint effort, more and more substantial preparation strategies and effective separation methods of CDs will be developed to promote wider applications of CDs.
Fangming Yang: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Peng Zhou: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Chunying Duan: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the National Major Scientific Instruments and Equipment Develop Project of China (2012YQ090197) and the National Science Foundation of China (21777017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106534.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon Dots: A new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications
ACS Cent. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, R. Li and B. Yang, Carbon Dots: A new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications, ACS Cent. Sci 6 (2020) 2179-2195. 10.1021/acscentsci.0c01306.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Truly fluorescent excitation-dependent carbon dots and their applications in multicolor cellular imaging and multidimensional sensing
Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Pan, S. Sun, A. Zhang, K. Jiang, L. Zhang, C. Dong, Q. Huang, A. Wu and H. Lin, Truly fluorescent excitation‐dependent carbon dots and their applications in multicolor cellular imaging and multidimensional sensing, Adv. Mater. 27 (2015) 7782–7787. 10.1002/adma.201503821.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiple color emission of solid-state hybrid material containing carbon dots and europium (III) complexes
J. Lumin.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Zhang, J. Yang and H. R. Li, Multiple color emission of solid-state hybrid material containing carbon dots and europium (III) complexes, Journal of Luminescence. 220 (2020) 116959. 10.1016/j.jlumin.2019.116959.

J. Mater. Chem. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. P. Fang, M. J. Wu, C. S. Huang, Z. Y. Liu, J. N. Liang and H. F. Zhang, Industrializable synthesis of narrow-dispersed carbon dots achieved by microwave-assisted selective carbonization of surfactants and their application as fluorescent nano-additives, J. Mater. Chem. A. 8 (2020) 21317. 10.1039/d0ta07252d.

Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng.
